# Transcrição: 03-Android-iOS Split Testing 

- **Fonte:** 03-Android-iOS Split Testing 
- **Duração:** 12:53
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Following the changes that Apple made in 2021 with iOS 14, one of the things we've been doing for each of our accounts is testing Android versus Apple. And one of the reasons for this is when we go into the testing phase, we wanna see what kind of difference there is in the data. So prior to this whole Apple problem, we would just run both devices, just target desktop and newsfeed, just to start the testing process. But now one of the common questions I get back is, you know, if we're doing testing, how do we know that the data is reliable? So one way to make the data reliable is to see what kind of difference there is between the two devices. Now, for the majority of you, your traffic will be on mobile, and the two mobile operating systems that most people use are iOS and Android. Therefore, why don't we test both devices with exactly the same audiences and targeting and creative, and see what kind of difference there is in tracking and performance. So this one here is a test we ran sometime in 2021, just to give you an illustration and example of the setup and what we're looking for. So with this particular one, it was just a test e-commerce store. We wanted to see what the difference was in the tracking and the numbers between iOS and Android. So as you can see, budget $100 a day, within each of the campaigns, we have exactly the same audiences. So the idea here is that we wanna test those audiences with creatives to see what performance will look like. So the creative, there's one creative in every audience. The audiences, the only difference we did on this one was to test different pixel events. So this one's optimized for purchase. This one is add payment info, view content, initiate checkout, add to cart, and there's another purchase one, which I think was just incorrectly set up, so we just relaunched that. But in both campaigns, we set up the same audiences with the same targeting and with the same five different optimization events. Now, if you're wondering why there's five different optimization events, then you probably haven't been through the account calibration test part of the training. So this is two things in one. One is we were running account calibration, and secondly, we wanted to do that across both devices. So in terms of setup, the only difference between the two campaigns is that one targets iOS devices only, the other one targets Android devices only. So there's zero chance of crossover unless someone's logged into two mobile devices with one Facebook user ID. So this is a safe test if you're worried about audience overlap and things like that. It's very rare that someone's gonna have both an Android phone and an iPhone

## [03:00 - 06:00]

unless they're into some kind of shady gangster stuff, but in most cases, we're good. So that was the setup. Just to quickly show you inside the ad set, the only difference is this specific mobile devices and operating system option. So this ad set you can see is part of the Android set of ad sets, it only targets Android. And this one here only targets iOS 14, oh, iOS. So we're going for any version here as well. So with that in place, so just bear in mind the settings, targeting, the optimizations, they're fairly like for like. We then ran this for around seven days. So you can see the budget spent there. I wasn't really worried about purchasing and things like that. This was more of a test of what the tracking looks like through the funnel. So if I go all the way back and look at my metrics in terms of clicks, so what I wanted to do is see what kind of difference there was in terms of what Facebook was tracking versus across the two different campaigns. So I'm gonna go back and find my CPMs. Let's have a look at, in fact, let's just go to the performance and clicks view here. So just as an overall view, they generated roughly the same number of clicks. The CPM, the cost was roughly the same and the number of impressions were roughly the same. So as far as the split test goes, this was a fair test. There was zero overlap, roughly the same number of impressions throughout. We had for our Android campaign a slightly higher click-through rate at 5.3% versus 4.7% for the iOS devices, which had a slight impact in cost per click, but nothing that I'm really worried about. So just bear that in mind as I look at these numbers. So from an ad performance point of view, there's really nothing between them, so that's good. So then we look at our funnel metrics. Now, obviously this is an e-commerce example, so I have an e-commerce funnel. You can test this for any funnel you're running and look at the data in a similar way as well. So website content views, this one here for Android, we can see that there were actually a lower number of Android content views, but then we look at website ads to cart and there's a significant drop. So 49 versus 67, just comparatively, initiate checkouts have actually dropped quite further. So we've got twice as many initiate checkouts, despite the fact we had fewer content views and we had a slightly higher number of website ads to cart. We had double the initiate checkouts. So the first thing you need to understand from here is there is tracking loss. There's no other reason why there'd be such a significant drop on the iOS, so this is the iOS campaign, versus the Android campaign.

## [06:00 - 09:02]

So that is my first insight as to what's going on. So if you've already set up your priority events and been through that part of the training, you should understand and know that the priority is based on what's important for you. So for most businesses, most e-com, whatever businesses running, in majority of cases, your number one event that you want tracked on iOS devices is gonna be purchase. For some of you, it might be call booking or something like that, but in most cases, purchase. And then you'll prioritize below that. So maybe for e-commerce, it would then be initiate checkout, add to cart, et cetera. So in that same way, if someone does come into a funnel and Facebook or Meta is only allowed to track one event, they'll track the event that was highest up on your priority list. So if your highest event is purchase, yet someone viewed content and they added to cart and they initiate checkout, then they purchase, Meta will only track one purchase. They won't track that there was also one initiate checkout, et cetera. So this is a good clue as to what's going on, because if you go all the way back, there were two purchases through both funnels. So far as the pixel is concerned, it did track two sales coming through both funnels. So if you look at it from the top level, so two purchases each, the ROAS was fairly similar, slightly better on Android. And the AOV obviously was slightly higher on Apple, sorry. So the ROAS was better. But if you looked purely at the metrics in the funnel, you'd assume that Android was performing better. So if you look here, cost per add to cart for Android was better at 11.75, for iPhone was 15, for Android getting people to the checkout was almost half. So let's say for example, we were optimizing based on these funnel metrics, which we have done in the past prior to Apple, and with the PICXL a lot more reliable, we could have previously looked at these numbers and said, you know what, this campaign here is not performing as well as this one here. However, because we've split that out, we know that this is Android and most likely more accurate. This is iPhone, iOS and likely less accurate. So we cannot rely on these, especially if both devices are targeted within the one ad set, because you can't split that out and look at that. Because if you now go to cost per acquisition, let's scroll across here and find our cost per acquisition. So it's 390 versus 393. Now ignore the fact that these are terribly bad cost per purchase numbers. The fact is they look identical because two purchases were generated through both funnels, but you can't see the funnel data. And this is what the test showed us, that there is a big difference for this particular account for the audience that we were targeting, which I believe was US 55 plus male and female

## [09:02 - 12:04]

that are interested in buying gifts for their grandchildren. So the audiences were very similar, is a US based audience, but we could see we were probably losing 100% or 50% of our tracking based on what we saw in iOS devices. Now the question you might be asking rightfully is, what do we do about this? What we did was that we focused all of our testing on Android only. Now, if there is a situation where you believe that your users and iPhone or iOS devices are very different to your Android users, then there might be an argument to say, test them together or in separate campaigns. But for us, we know that the metrics are very, very similar. We've just proven it. In fact, if you look at these numbers here, both generated two purchases, both were very similar to RIS. But actually, and by the way, this is something we've seen across this particular account quite a lot, is that the average order value for an iOS user is generally for this account a lot higher than Android. And you may find the same as well. So I would never say don't target our iOS devices unless obviously there was a good reason not to, but from our testing perspective. So using the graduation testing process, for us it made sense to test on Android only, because we knew that when we went through to the prospecting stage at phase three, the performance when we added iOS into that would be much better. So you might ask, well, when we come to phase three and we start running prospecting campaigns, should we run them completely separately? In my view, no, because that can cause more overhead and more kind of challenges with getting stability with the algorithm, because here's the thing, even though you're losing out, initiate checkouts on the iPhone device, if you're all in the same ad set, you're still getting them from Android, you can split them out. If you want better tracking, then you can absolutely put this into your prospecting campaign and split out iOS devices from Android and use Android as your base to understand what's working. What I mean by that is, let's say, for example, you've split out your campaigns in your prospecting, your scaling campaigns, and you've got iOS in one, Android in the other, you're not really gonna worry about overlap, but you can use the Android campaign as a base to understand what's working and what's not. So you might have an iOS device campaign, where you look at it and your cost-paradise card seems twice as high as your target. Let's say your target is $10 and iOS is trailing at $20 at a card rate, but then you look at your Android performance and it's at $11 or $10 or thereabouts, and you're like, it's exactly the same audience, the same ad, the same land or everything else. Therefore, I'm gonna assume it is also working on iOS devices

## [12:04 - 12:53]

because it's working on Android, so you can absolutely use that as well. So there's a lot you can gain, but the first thing I encourage you to do is to test your Android versus iOS campaigns, your ad sets, your event targeting, so that you can see this data for yourself. Now, I spent $100 a day not worrying too much about conversions. You might not have $1,500 to run a test like this. You can do it on lower budgets. You don't need to have all the multiple ad sets that we're running. You can literally just do it with one ad set and one ad inside that ad set, and that's fine as well. But this allows you to just see how different the data looks, and then you can make an informed decision off the back of that on how you can optimize going forward.
